data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Caution Is Warranted
data.description:
A couple of things before I start:

  1. I have been away from Seeking Alpha for a while, but I intend to become more active.
  2. My goal is to format my articles using a consistent format, the same as I would for my various institutional roles. Essentially, you are the client and should expect consistency. That said, bear with me as I get my format down.

With that out of the way, let's get busy.

Company Description

Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC or "Blue Owl") is the second-largest publicly traded Business Development Company ("BDC") by market capitalization ($6.2B) and among the largest by total assets ($17.6B as of Q3 2025). The company provides direct lending solutions to upper middle-market U.S. companies, focusing on senior secured first-lien investments (74% of portfolio).

Investment Thesis and Recommendation

Blue Owl and other BDCs are predominantly used as an income investment with the possibility of some capital appreciation. Blue Owl (and other BDCs) invest primarily in senior secured loans to non-investment-grade companies. While this is similar to a bank loan fund, Blue Owl and BDCs invest in private companies, with the majority of these companies being private equity portfolio companies (often called "sponsored companies").

As a result of the income focus, the primary focus of analysis is the portfolio income generated and the quality of the underlying portfolio. To this end:

  • Portfolio quality has declined (albeit modestly) sequentially and year-over-year in terms of non-accrual loans,
  • Portfolio yield has fallen (due mainly to a decline in SOFR as well as some spread compression),
  • The $0.37 dividend was not covered by net investment income, which also resulted in no supplemental dividend.

While Blue Owl has shown themselves to be one of the better firms in the private credit space, it is likely that the dividend will be reduced. At this juncture the market appears to have this priced in and, as we will discuss later, the company has tried to guide. The stock now trades at a 17% discount to Q3 '25 NAV and a P/B of 0.85%. This discount is the highest in the peer group (looking at similarly sized firms such as Golub (GBDC), Ares (ARCC), Sixth Street (TSLX), and Blackstone (BXSL)).

While the current price (and P/NAV) may already be discounting this, I don't see any rush to enter into the name right now. If you have held the name through the drop, I do not see a reason to sell it at this juncture, as the majority of the damage has been done. This is said with the understanding that if the dividend is cut, there will be a negative reaction in the market (there is nearly always a knee-jerk reaction). One must also realize there are systemic pressures on the space right now, in private credit (GP/LP), interval/evergreen funds, and BDCs. The concern, generally, is that asset quality is declining, too much capital is chasing deals, and the validity of portfolio marks and therefore NAV.

Bottom Line: HOLD. For people considering entering the name, I would wait until Q4 earnings come out on Feb 18, 2026, to get a feel for portfolio performance and dividend policy/coverage. The alternative is to "leg into" the position and not take your full size immediately. Honestly, not sure this needs to be done for any other reason than "gotta own that yield".

The Manager

OBDC is managed by Blue Owl Credit Advisors LLC., an indirect affiliate of Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL or "Blue Owl Capital") and part of Blue Owl’s Credit platform. Blue Owl consists of three investment platforms:

  1. Credit ($152B),
  2. GP Strategic Capital ($69B), which primarily focuses on acquiring equity stakes in, or providing debt financing to, large, multi-product private equity and private credit firms, and
  3. Real Assets ($75B), which primarily focuses on the strategies of net lease real estate, real estate credit, and digital infrastructure, which focuses on acquiring, financing, developing, and operating data centers and related digital infrastructure assets.

Blue Owl currently has over $295 billion in AUM across the three strategies.

Blue Owl was formed in 2016 as Owl Rock, a direct lending firm. Since the merger with Dyal (GP stakes) in 2021, and subsequent listing, the firm's assets have grown significantly and have broadened out into nearly all facets of private investing. Importantly, Blue Owl Capital (formerly known as Owl Rock Capital Corp.) went public in 2019, an exit strategy for the original institutional investors.

Portfolio Profile

The portfolio is intentionally weighted toward the top of the capital structure to provide downside protection. Senior secured investments represent 80% of the total portfolio at fair value (1st and 2nd lien).

OBDC maintains high diversification, seeking to limit any single industry to under 20% of the portfolio. The top ten industries represent 62.2% of the total. The largest industry exposure is internet software and services, at 11.5%.

Portfolio Quality

OBDC's portfolio consists mainly of loans to middle-market companies with EBITDA between $25 million and $500 million annually and/or annual revenue of $125 million to $5 billion. Within this space, they predominantly focus on investing in upper-middle-market businesses (EBITDA of $50mm+), as the transactions can be larger and have a greater impact.

Importantly, 74% of OBDC's lending is in the form of "first lien", or priority debt. An investor must realize, however, that this is not always what it seems. Within the first lien, you van also have "First Lien, First Out" and "First Lien, Last Out" dynamics where there is a waterfall payment structure within the first lien bucket. This is often seen within a "unitranche" structure, where the borrower sees one tranche, but there are waterfall agreements between the lenders. Also important, 52% of OBDC's first lien loans are unitranche, with no specification of FLFO or FLLO. Not all first liens are equal.

OBDC is also increasing exposure to asset-based lending ("ABL") - it was 12% of new investments in Q3, up from 1% the prior year. ABL is worth a brief digression. Asset-based lending is considered "specialty finance", which is used for helping companies fund their businesses to consumer credit cards. This segment has been making some folks a bit nervous since the First Brands debacle, where receivables were pledged multiple times. When this is added to a deteriorating consumer profile (higher risk in the consumer space), lower collateral values, and general angst over underwriting standards, there will be an impact on the share price of ABL providers, such as OBDC.

Generally speaking, OBDC has a high-quality portfolio that is well diversified in terms of borrowers and industries. Portfolio performance has been decent, but there has been an increase in non-accruals, which bear watching, but they are not overly serious right now (again, the Q4 call will provide more clarity).

Liquidity

The company utilizes a mix of funding sources, with a strategic emphasis on unsecured borrowings to maintain financial flexibility.

OBDC's debt distribution is also well balanced.

Dividend Stability

Ultimately, the management and portfolio quality will affect that which income investors really care about: income. Distributable income will be a function of:

  1. Portfolio yield,
  2. portfolio credit quality, and
  3. Form of payment

The yield on the portfolio has been going down for two primary reasons:

  1. Floating Rate Exposure. As of 9/30/25, 97% of the portfolio is floating rate. As the Fed's policy rate goes down, SOFR goes down and the portfolio yield goes down, as shown below. As well, the risk premium, or "spread" has been steadily dropping as capital chases product (the same thing is happening in the investment grade, high yield, and bank loan space).

There are currently 2-3 further policy rate cuts expected this year, which means that the yields on the investments will fall further.

The result can then be estimated by the company's sensitivity test (from the 10Q).

OBDC recognizes this and has tried to guide investors towards the dividend reduction as a result of yield. From their earnings call transcript:

We have a strong performing portfolio. And our dividend levels for investors who are newer to the stock were lower in a lower rate environment. If you went back to when rates were at 3%, our dividend was about $0.33. So I think you can look at where our rates were at a 3% environment at $0.33 and get a sense of the order of magnitude of what we might consider.

OBDC did not earn their dividend last quarter (about $0.01 shy), but the company can "plug" the shortfall with their "spillover" (undistributed taxable income that a BDC carries forward) income. OBDC has a meaningful amount of spillover income, which can help stabilize the dividend. From their Q3 transcript:

As we have previously reported, our spillover income remains healthy at approximately $0.31 per share and supported our base dividend this quarter.

Portfolio quality and structure will determine if the underlying investments are paying the BDC, which is why we look at non-accruals. A deterioration in credit quality often leads to non-accruals. As mentioned above, there has been a slight deterioration in quality (non-accruals higher and what looks like a minor shift from class 4 to class 5 investments), but it is not unmanageable at this juncture.

How the loans pay is another factor. Many of the loans issued by the company allow debtors to pay in kind (PIK). This means that instead of paying cash interest (or dividend), the debtor can simply increase the amount of their loan. This is still considered income from an accounting/reporting standpoint, but it is not cash that can be distributed to shareholders. The amount of investment income that is PIK income has been dropping over the last year after reaching 13.5% in 2024.

Comparisons

While there are many BDCs, I tend to group Blue Owl in with the peer group I mentioned in the beginning.

An overview of the peer group statistics (as of 9/30/25) shows that OBDC is relatively similar to the peer group in all but non-accruals (they are higher) and dividend coverage (they are lower). Note that OBDC and ARCC also have lower portfolio yields than the remainder of the peer group.

Due in part because of the higher non-accruals (and in part because of issues surrounding Blue Owl Capital), Blue Owl has underperformed every member of the peer group with the exception of BXSL.

Despite having the highest dividend yield (which often attracts capital and supports the shares):

Which has led to the largest discount to book value:

Performance for the sector has been difficult, yields notwithstanding. As we said earlier, the systemic pressure on the space allows for a patient entry into the space.

Errata

A discussion on BDCs is not complete without a mention of fees paid on the product. BDCs are (for the most part) publicly traded private credit. As such, they have a fee structure consistent with private assets. The fees consist of:

  1. Management Fee,
  2. Income Incentive Fee, and
  3. Capital gains Fee.

There is no such thing as a free lunch. I might actually put out a note on the fee structure of BDCs and compare a number of BDCs, as I believe you have to understand the economics of an investment.

I realize this has been lengthy, and I appreciate those who read to the end. Going forward, when I write about BDCs, many of the terms will have been defined and the concepts outlined, so it won't be as painful.

data.title: Under A Warsh Fed, Expect A Thoughtful Policy Approach
data.description:
President Trump has announced his intention to nominate Kevin Warsh to become the next chair of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. We believe Warsh will be confirmed by the Senate and serve as an effective, thoughtful Fed chair. He brings intriguing ideas on ways to change and ideally improve how the Fed operates. We also remain confident in the outlook for Fed independence, which appears to have broad support not just in markets but in Congress as well.

Warsh is well-known, respected, and accomplished. After earning a law degree from Harvard, he began his career on Wall Street, then joined the National Economic Council under President George W. Bush, and then served with distinction as a Fed governor from 2006 to 2011. This Fed tenure included the global financial crisis (GFC), when Warsh was a valued liaison between the Fed and financial market executives. Since leaving the Fed, Warsh has split his time as a distinguished visiting fellow at the Hoover Institution and as an adviser to legendary investor Stan Druckenmiller.

Warsh’s views on the Fed balance sheet

Over the past 15 years, Warsh has written and spoken extensively about Fed policy. He has raised concerns about the size and composition of the Fed’s large balance sheet. He has also questioned the central bank’s reliance on forward guidance – which he believes is excessive and sends confusing signals about future monetary policy – along with what he views as the Fed’s failure to anchor policy formulation and communication to policy rules that are less subject to meeting-by-meeting discretion.

More recently, Warsh has argued for a new “Treasury–Fed accord” that could, depending on the details, provide over time a framework for the Fed working in tandem with the Treasury – and perhaps also with the housing agencies Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac – to shrink the size of its balance sheet. This is noteworthy because the Fed is now once again growing its balance sheet through reserve management purchases of T-bills, having ended its quantitative tightening (QT) program (i.e., policies to reduce the balance sheet) in December 2025. Under Warsh’s approach, a new framework may also include the Fed gradually shifting the composition of its balance sheet to a much shorter duration than at present, as was the practice before the GFC.

Warsh's views on interest rates and Fed communications

As for Fed interest rate policy, Warsh has recently argued that the Fed has been “backward-looking” and too slow to cut rates. Thus, he would very likely favor delivering at minimum the two 25-basis point cuts for 2026 indicated in the December 2025 Fed economic projections (the “dot plot”). These cuts, which are largely priced into markets, would bring the federal funds rate down to a range of 3%–3.25%.

The Federal Open Market Committee (or FOMC, the policy-setting group of Fed governors and regional presidents) that Warsh will inherit is divided over when, how much, and how often to cut the policy rate. That said, under the Fed’s and PIMCO’s baseline scenario, we believe Warsh will be able to garner at least the seven votes he needs on the 12-person FOMC to deliver those two cuts this year – and perhaps a third cut that would bring the fed funds rate down to 2.75%–3%, which notably is the median of the FOMC’s current estimate of neutral.

Beyond those next two or three rate cuts, we believe Warsh may be more wary, depending on the inflation outlook. He understands that longer-term bond and mortgage yields incorporate not only the Fed policy rate but also expected inflation, and he may hesitate to press for additional rate cuts if measures of expected inflation were to rise much above current levels. Current expectations for long-run inflation remain consistent with the Fed’s 2% inflation target.

Perhaps the biggest difference investors may notice between the Warsh Fed and the Jerome Powell, Janet Yellen, and Ben Bernanke Feds is in communication policy. Warsh, based on his writings since leaving the Fed, may be much less likely to rely on extensive forward guidance about the future path of interest rates, especially during “normal” times – as is the case today – when interest rates are not pinned at the zero bound. Warsh can point to precedent: Paul Volcker for eight years and Alan Greenspan for his first 17 years chaired very successfully a Fed that delivered price stability and supported strong growth with little if any forward guidance as we know it today on the future path of policy rates. However, financial markets (and Fed watchers) since then have become quite accustomed – and critics would say addicted to – Fed “open mouth” operations, and the transition to a new communication regime may be bumpy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Mission Produce: Avocados Consolidate
data.description:
In the fall of 2023, I believed that Mission Produce (AVO) was not ripe for a position. The avocado business suffered a downturn and was actually posting losses. Despite having a leading position in this growing market, I feared the commodity element of the business and thus the risks involved.

Forwarding more than two years later, shares have done quite well, having risen some 50%, in tandem with the market at large. This comes as the business remains a commodity play certainly, although Mission Produce aims to tackle this in part by improving its market positioning, with an intended acquisition of Calavo Growers (CVGW).

Mostly About Avocados

Mission Produce was founded back in 1983 as a leader in worldwide fresh produce, with avocados and mangos delivered to retailers, wholesale companies, and foodservice companies across the globe. The company is vertically integrated with operations ranging from farms to logistics, ripening, and packaging solutions, allowing the business to supply its customers year-round. Some 60% of sales are distributed directly to retailers, complemented by other distribution channels.

The company went public in 2020, and by now (that is, 2024) generates some $1.2 billion in sales, of which nearly 90% is tied to avocados, complemented by blueberries and mangos, with revenues having increased some 150% over the past decade.

Notably, demand for avocados has been on the rise, driven by general concern of consumers about their health, while avocados are considered healthy food. Consumer demand for these is even more pronounced for Hispanics and Gen-Z, only adding to demand for these products. This comes as consumption currently is still relatively modest, averaging at 9 pounds per person in the US and 4 pounds in the European regions.

The issue with the business is that of uneven margins, with weather and pricing having major impacts. Over the past decade, the company has had margins come in anywhere between break-even and low-double digits, but no sustainable trends are visible here.

When the business went public in 2020, a $12 stock was valued at $830 million, excluding a $60 million net debt load. This valuation was applied to a business that generated $883 million in sales in 2019, as operating profits boomed to $106 million that year, with earnings topping a dollar per share.

These earnings plunged in 2021, as the company was loss-making when I looked at the situation in 2023, with shares down to $8. The operating performance made it difficult to build conviction, certainly with net debt being on the increase.

Recovering

What followed was a recovery in the 2024 results, with that momentum carrying on in the 2025 results. Annual revenues for the year rose by 13% to $1.39 billion, as notably non-avocado sales grew rapidly by 37% to $195 million. Operating profits of $65 million were flat in dollar terms compared to 2024, resulting in mid-single-digit operating margins, with net earnings of $37 million being equivalent to $0.53 per share.

Net debt has been very minimal at around $30 million, as 71 million shares now trade at $13, for a $950 million enterprise valuation, basically at par compared to 2020. Earnings power is relatively minimal to support this share price, with shares trading at a mid-twenty times earnings multiple; this is demanding for a business posting margins in the middle of its historical range.

So far, so good, as the company warned of lower results in the first quarter of 2026, with a 10% increase in avocado volumes seen resulting in a 25% fall in pricing.

A Huge Move

The standalone investment case was thrown upside down in January 2026, with the company announcing a deal to acquire Calavo Growers (CVGW). The deal is valued at $430 million; that is on an enterprise basis, with investors in Calavo obtaining $27 per share for their ownership. This is comprised of a $14.85 per share cash component and 0.9790 shares of Mission being issued for every share outstanding in Calavo.

With 71 million shares outstanding as of the third quarter, and Calavo's shareholders as a group seen as having 19.7% of the pro forma share count, I see a pro forma share count up to 88 million shares. That suggests about 17 million shares to be issued, which makes for a $200 million equity component at the prevailing price of $12 at the time of the announcement. This suggests that net debt might increase by some $230 million to $260 million.

The idea and rationale behind the deal is that of scale and, related to that, synergies, with these seen at $25 million within 18 months post-closing, those being very significant numbers. Other opportunities and goals include that of growing the avocado dominance, further diversification between fruits and regions, and further vertical integration of the business.

With Mission valued at $950 million, the business is valued at 0.7 times sales reported in 2025 and around 8.5 times EBITDA. Calavo is valued at $430 million in this deal, equivalent to just less than 0.7 times sales. Driven by much lower EBITDA margins, it is valued at 10.5 times EBITDA, although those synergies could be quite substantial here, and Calavo operates with net cash holdings.

Pro forma EBITDA is seen around $152 million; that is ex-synergies, which should be okay to manage a roughly quarter of a billion debt load, certainly as synergies kick in.

What Now?

The reality is that this combination looks pretty solid, with Mission growing the business by around 50%, gaining more scale and diversification. The issue is that the business, even though it becomes larger, remains commodity-like, now with some debt employed as well. The real issue is that even in 2025, which was a pretty representative year, earnings were only stuck around half a dollar, making a valuation around $13 per share not necessarily cheap.

Investors have not been enjoying buybacks or dividends, as this deal will prevent that from happening anytime soon. While the move makes perfect sense for the business, and the exchange ratios are largely fair, the issue is that of a lack of sustainable shareholder value creation in the past.

Valuations are simply not cheap enough for me to see a meaningful discount to intrinsic value, and to create commitment on the shares here.

data.title: Granite Point Mortgage Trust: Discount To Book Has Bottomed, A Rerate On The Cards
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome J. Collinski as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Variant view: Granite Point Mortgage Trust (GPMT) has been a falling knife for years, but I’m calling a bottom here based on the recovery in the underlying commercial real estate markets paired with signs of portfolio stabilization. I believe the discount to book will shrink during 2026 as the market rerates GPMT higher based on improving fundamentals, resulting in a materially higher share price.

Background

GPMT is part of the mortgage REIT (mREIT) sector. It originates floating rate senior secured real estate loans, and manages a portfolio of $1.8B as of Q3 2025. It is down ~80% since 2021 highs because a large portion of its loan portfolio was commercial real estate (CRE), including 42% office space, which plummeted in value post-2021. GPMT tends to underwrite about 65% loan to value on properties, meaning that they generally shouldn’t lose money until property values fall more than 35%. Historically that was a safe margin, but commercial real estate, especially office properties, did fall more than 35% in recent years. In some areas they fell 50%+. This has caused GPMT’s book value (BV) to fall from $17/share in 2021 to about $8/share today, as loan losses accumulated.

Commercial Real Estate is Recovering, and GPMT’s Book Value Losses are Slowing

However, after a bumpy ride down, GPMT’s portfolio is stabilizing. Most of their troubled loans have been resolved, and the office space itself has been improving. Companies have been pushing workers back into offices, and some offices have been converted into other uses. Office vacancy rates are expected to fall further in 2026, and asking rents have stabilized. (Colliers 2026 CRE Market Outlook) Peak stress on GPMT’s loan portfolio is likely behind us.

We can see this recovery manifesting in the trend of GPMT’s portfolio activity and write downs. Starting in 2020, based on new accounting regulations, GPMT began reporting a metric called ‘current expected credit loss’ (CECL) that is essentially the management’s forecast of future loan impairments. GPMT’s reported BV is net of the CECL reserve. You can see that by late 2022, the CECL reserve was growing consistently each quarter (loss expectations increasing), and book value accordingly shrank. Both metrics have been stabilizing in recent quarters, however, with GPMT reporting book value declines of only $0.23, $0.25, and $0.05 per share over the past three quarters, respectively. That compares to a peak q/q decline of $1.77 per share in Q1 2024. The CECL reserve has been shrinking lately as impaired loans have been resolved without getting replaced by new impairments.

We can see that CECL reserves as a percentage of book value peaked in mid-2024, and have been trending down since then. The discount to book value, which was trending down for years, is now closer to flat over the past ~12 months. It bottomed around ~20% in April 2025 during the broader market selloff.

Management struggled with long-term loss forecasting for the first few years of this downturn. However, in the short term, GPMT has been fairly accurate at CECL forecasting. Consider the five loans they resolved in 2025:

So, while management struggled to foresee more troubled loans emerging (long-term), they have been good at pricing the losses expected from already impaired loans. If we think most of the troubled loan emergence is behind us, most of the surprise BV deterioration should be too. Investors should still expect some amount of additional BV deterioration from here, though, either due to slightly negative earnings before originations (portfolio growth) restart, or a bit more loan underperformance emerging. The real question is, does GPMT deserve to be trading around ~30% of BV when most of the BV losses are behind us? Let’s look at where things stand today.

GPMT is Making Progress Towards Resolving its Remaining High-Risk Assets

As of Q3 2025 earnings, GPMT had 3 ‘risk 5’ loans remaining and two real estate owned (REO) assets it took possession of as a means of resolving previous high-risk loans. ‘Risk 5’ is GPMT’s highest internal risk rating, which it specifies as meaning ‘loss likely’. GPMT hasn’t had so few ‘risk 5’ loans since 2022, when the portfolio stress was just emerging. Loans sitting at ‘risk 5’ already have specific CECL reserves allocated to them.

Here are the specifics of those 3 ‘risk 5’ loans:

Regarding the Tempe, AZ Hotel, management had this to say on the Q3 earnings call:

“The property had been under contract with a hard deposit at a price well in excess of our loan amount. However, that sale is now on hold. And in combination with the property's performance, we felt it was prudent to change this rating.”

My take: It sounds like this loan was on track to be resolved without a loss to GPMT, but the transaction got held up. Even if the resolution does ultimately end up resulting in a loss, I would expect it to be minor.

Regarding the Chicago, IL office property:

“During the third quarter, we had a partial resolution related to the $79 million Chicago office loan with the sale of the upper floor office space while retaining the ground floor retail. Working with our borrower and the new buyer, the zoning change of the upper floors to residential use was approved by the city of Chicago after a lengthy process. The sale resulted in net proceeds of $3.4 million, which we used to pay down the loan to about $76 million.

Since the pandemic, the bulk of the value has been in the retail component. And now with the sale of the upper floor office space, our remaining collateral is the ground floor retail on the Magnificent Mile. As a result of the office sale, the story is less complicated for potential buyers as we proceed towards the ultimate resolution, which should occur over the next few quarters. Following this sale, the loan was reclassified from office to retail.”

My take: Carving out the previous office component and reclassifying the remaining holding as retail should make this property significantly easier to sell. I would not be surprised to see it resolve in the next quarter or two, and at a reduced risk of surprise markdown.

Finally, the Minneapolis, MN property:

“Regarding the $93 million Minneapolis office loan, as previously disclosed, we anticipate a longer resolution time line given the persistent local market challenges. We are seeing the beginnings of the long-awaited return to office mandates in Minneapolis. While it seems premature to call this a recovery, the trends are slowly moving in the right direction.”

My take: This one could stick around for a while, since the local market is still struggling here. Still, being down to a single 'risk 5’ loan within a few quarters would represent major progress for GPMT.

GPMT’s two REO assets are the result of previous loan resolutions. On June 27, 2024, GPMT acquired the legal title to an office property in Maynard, MA (a Boston suburb) with a carrying value of $35.7M. In January 2025, GPMT took possession of an office property in Miami Beach, FL, with a carrying value of $71M.

The Florida office property is the less worrisome of the two. It is a ‘class A’ office asset, and the Miami office market has been rebounding strongly:

Source: Miami Office Market Report – Q4 2025

I am hopeful they will be able to sell this property relatively soon due to the underlying market health.

The Boston area property has already been on the books for 5 quarters now, and may need to wait longer. While Greater Boston office values are rising (chart below), suburban office values are still near cycle lows. However, management reported “positive leasing successes” at this property, and say they are exploring “several value-enhancing repositioning opportunities”.

Source: Boston Office Market Report, Q4 2025

These two REO assets were valued primarily via DCF when they were obtained, and will carry some valuation uncertainty until they are sold. We do, however, have a recent datapoint that serves as an endorsement of their REO accounting. On June 23, 2025, GPMT sold an office property in Phoenix, AZ that it had held outright since May of 2023. The property had a carrying value of $16.4M at the time, and was sold for $16.7M, generating a $0.3M net gain.

Altogether, I expect to see GPMT’s collection of remaining ‘risk 5’ loans and REO assets to continue shrinking in 2026. They may not all be resolved in the near term, but as long as the total continues ticking down, the market should reward them with a less aggressive discount.

GPMT’s Management Knows How to Avoid Worst-Case Outcomes

I have been following GPMT for five years, and feel its management team has performed fairly well - all things considered. The drop it experienced in 2020 was essentially a liquidity crisis relating to short term recourse debt, collateralized by assets that suddenly had little to no liquidity during the COVID crash. Effectively, the whole mREIT sector was facing margin calls. Some of GPMT’s peers faced forced liquidations at distressed pricing, and experienced significant book value losses. GPMT, however, managed to juggle their assets and source additional non-recourse funding, emerging from the 2020 crisis largely unscathed by comparison.

Then when office valuation stress began emerging in 2022, management halted new loan originations, and switched into full defensive mode. As untroubled loans matured, they retained the returned principal, building liquidity. They also decreased the dividend by 80%, from $0.25/Q to $0.05/Q as the downturn progressed. In 2024, evaluating that things were starting to stabilize, management began buying back shares to capitalize on the depressed valuation. Since Q2 2024, GPMT has spent $13.2M buying back 4.55M shares at a weighted average price of $2.91/share. These repurchases have lifted BV by $0.52/share, buffering some of the BV declines. GPMT did not disclose any additional share repurchases during Q3 2025, so this tailwind appears to have paused for now.

While there were certainly errors made given the significant realized book value losses, GPMT management was hardly alone in missing the sharp repricing that hit commercial real estate valuations. What they have been good at is avoiding the worst-case outcomes in crisis situations. That’s important in the current context.

GPMT’s Discount to Book Value Looks Too Extreme

When times are good, mREITs tend to trade at around 1x their book values; book value is often a good net asset value representation in this space. BXMT for example (a larger, more diversified commercial mREIT) currently trades around 0.9x book value. GPMT last traded around 1x BV in 2020. After an initial drop during Covid, GPMT had previously recovered to about 80% of its BV of ~$17/share by mid-2021. It then started forecasting loan losses due to delinquencies on its commercial real estate loans, so the recovery faltered. GPMT currently reports a BV of $7.94/share, including a $2.82 write down for ongoing loan resolutions and expected credit losses. GPMT’s BV declined by $0.05 (-0.6%) in the most recent quarter.

I’m building in some room for additional BV deterioration, and setting a one-year forward price target of $3.85/share based on 55% of a forward BV of $7/share. This represents a $44.6M ($0.94/share) buffer against future losses and negative earnings. For reference, GPMT’s BV declined by $0.53/share combined over the past 3 quarters. In the most optimistic of outcomes, GPMT could recover some BV through better-than-expected resolutions, buybacks, and portfolio growth, and trade as high as $8-9 after a full recovery.

While I am not counting on it, the deep discount GPMT trades at also makes it a potential acquisition target.

Risks

The most obvious risk is that additional portfolio stress emerges, with additional loans going delinquent and reversing the current trend of stabilization. In addition to negatively impacting BV, this would also likely push out the timeline to the market rerating GPMT’s discount to BV. A negative macro event like a recession could increase this risk.

Even without additional loan delinquencies, there is timing risk here, as it is hard to forecast exactly how quickly the market will take to rerate GPMT higher. GPMT recovered rapidly in 2020, but it was an acute risk it faced at the time, and the current challenge has been much more drawn out. The recovery might also be drawn out as the market waits to see stronger signs of portfolio health. A tightening of financial conditions could also slow resolutions or pressure earnings.

While relatively small compared to other forces acting on GPMT, owning real estate assets outright also adds some additional operational risk to earnings. Until all owned real estate assets are sold, GPMT is temporarily in the business of managing properties directly, with $5.8M in owned real estate expenses in Q3 2025 vs. $3.6M in owned real estate leasing income.

Conclusion

In summary, many signs point to a page being turned in GPMT’s story. Commercial real estate, and offices in particular, appears to have bottomed out in 2024/2025 and its fundamentals are now improving. This is being reflected in GPMT’s portfolio health, with fewer loans becoming distressed. With portfolio stress decreasing, GPMT’s book value is stabilizing. If these trends continue, GPMT’s discount to book value will shrink. I believe that its discount to book value likely bottomed in 2025, and the next chapter will be a rerating higher. Catalysts will be further quarterly reports and mid-quarter business updates, showing continued progress towards portfolio stability and eventually fresh portfolio growth.

For those who are intrigued by GPMT’s story but reluctant to hold the equity, GPMT has a series A preferred share issuance currently yielding ~8.6%, plus ~23% potential price appreciation if it returned to par value ($25).

data.title: Chicago PMI Jumps To 2-Year High In January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Chicago Purchasing Managers’ Index (Chicago Business Barometer) jumped to its highest level in over two years in January, rising 11.3 points to 54.0. This reading came in above the projected 43.5 and marks the first month regional business activity has expanded since the end of 2023.

Background on Chicago PMI

The Chicago PMI assesses the business conditions and the economic health of the manufacturing sector in the Chicago region. A value above 50.0 indicates expanding manufacturing activity, while a value below 50.0 indicates contracting manufacturing activity.

Weak readings have often preceded broader economic downturns. Looking at the index's historical performance, the current level of 54.0 is lower than where the index stood at the start of 2 of the 7 recessions since its inception.

A Closer Look Since 2000

Examining data since 2000, the index has undergone fluctuations, often aligning with broader economic cycles. Given its persistent weakness, the current level reinforces concerns about ongoing softness in the manufacturing sector.

Chicago PMI vs. ISM Manufacturing Index

Let's compare the Chicago PMI with the more popular national ISM manufacturing index. Over the 673 months of available data, the Chicago PMI has been higher 68% of the time. However, despite this frequent outperformance, its historical average of 54.2 is only slightly higher than the ISM’s 52.4.

It's clear the two indices tend to move together; however, it’s important to note that the ISM Manufacturing Index is reported with a one-month lag.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ares Capital Becomes More Attractive As Pricing Changes
data.description:
Private lending has been the talk of the town over the past several years. In the post pandemic era, money moved fast and private credit took center stage as the hot new source of capital for companies of all shapes and sizes. When people think of private credit, they typically think junk bonds, sponsored companies, or distressed investments. In the era of modern specialized finance, it’s not so simple. Perhaps the best example is the recent deal between Meta Platforms (META), Blue Owl (OWL), and PIMCO to finance the construction of the infamous Hyperion facility.

As the private lending segment continues to develop into a trillion dollar business, cracks are beginning to show. Recently, I published an article talking about systemic issues that are hitting business development companies and securitized credit. Today, we will dive back into Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), the largest business development company.

As a flurry of concerns begin to threaten to continued success of business development companies, a lot rides on the largest market participant. Beyond being a fan favorite, ARCC accounts for large portions of BDC funds like the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), the largest BDC ETF. ARCC is the largest holding and accounts for 15% of the fund’s assets. It’s time to follow up on recent coverage and talk about a new era for BDCs.

Review of Prior Coverage

Nearly one year ago, I published an article covering ARCC. In the article, I took a dive into the fund’s business model and manager. We talked about the wide diversification across borrowers and industries as well as how ARCC leverages the broader Ares Management (ARES) platform. The fund has historically outperformed the sector by a material margin.

In the article, I covered the fund’s Q4 and fiscal year-end earnings report. Perhaps the highlight of the earnings release was the announcement that the long term CEO was stepping down. This set the stage for an uncertain year where an emergent CEO was tasked with carrying the torch. Large shoes to fill may have been an understatement.

A focal point of my coverage of ARCC has been the company’s explosive growth. Over the past several years, ARCC has issued billions of dollars of debt and equity to keep pace with the booming private credit market. The company had added billions of dollars to the market capitalization in just several years.

In the article, I called ARCC expensive. I highlighted that the fund was trading at a material premium to their book value.

ARCC’s net asset value is $19.89 per share, a record level for the fund. Currently, ARCC is trading around $23 per share, which corresponds to a significant premium to book value. As displayed below, ARCC is still trading at a premium to book value of nearly 20%.

I felt as though ARCC’s price was more likely to return to net asset value than vice versa. Over the past year, shares have declined by around 13%, while book value has climbed modestly.

Total return, including dividends, has been negative over the past twelve months. Bad news hitting the private credit sphere has largely spoiled the party for ARCC as well as the broader sector. The question is whether these risks are enough to affect ARCC.

Corporate Activity

BDCs are currently under pressure with today’s interest rate environment. To jog our memories, business development companies operate much like REITs in their barebones business models. They borrow money in the form of either debt or equity, then invest that money, hoping to earn more than the cost of that borrowed capital. The excess return net of operational expenses is the entrepreneurial profit delivered to investors.

In the case of BDCs, the company issues capital through stock and a variety of forms of debt. Typically, these financing sources are fixed rate with either a level (or increasing) dividend or a fixed coupon rate in the case of unsecured debt. Without diving too deep into the worlds of preferreds and baby bonds, these are also typically fixed rate. This capital is typically lent to borrowers at floating rates through loans that are priced over SOFR. In the case of ARCC, around 70% of the portfolio are straightforward, floating rate loans.

Connecting this to today’s environment, we see trouble. Over the past several years, SOFR increased significantly, which supercharged the top line cash flow to business development companies like ARCC. In the short run, this means that the company earned more cash without doing much of anything. However, rates have remained higher for longer than expected. Today, BDCs like ARCC are beginning to refinance their maturing debt at much higher rates.

For example, ARCC just issued $750 million in bonds with a coupon rate of 5.25%. These bonds have a five-year maturity. If we are to look at historical investor reports, ARCC had $1.2 billion in secured debt maturing in January 2026 at an interest rate of 3.875%. The recent refinancing represents a movement of around 150 basis points upwards in interest expense. While this single financing is not material to bottom line financial results, it pairs with a troubling trend on the top line.

It appears as though short term financing rates are likely to fall on account of pressure from the President. This week, the Fed announced they intend to keep the federal funds rate steady. Shortly thereafter, Trump announced his pick for chairman of the Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh.

President Trump announced on Friday that he was nominating Kevin M. Warsh to serve as the next chair of the Federal Reserve, positioning the former central bank governor to take a pivotal role in steering an institution that has faced a barrage of attacks from the administration over its reluctance to more aggressively lower interest rates.

In a post on Truth Social, Mr. Trump praised Mr. Warsh, saying, “He will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best.”

Trump has been intentional about pressuring the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates. For a company like ARCC, an immediate drop in interest rates would have a material impact on top line cash flow to the business.

Trouble In Paradise

Changes in market dynamics and macroeconomics will affect each BDC differently. Recently, we have already seen issues begin to hit different companies like FS KKR Capital (FSK) and Gladstone Capital (GLAD), both of which have cut their dividends and seen their valuations hammered.

Even the best in the business have been impacted by the news from companies like First Brands, which have wide exposure in many private lending vehicles.

Paired with speculation around deals like that between META and OWL, BDC investors are on their toes. While ARCC is yet to show signs of weakness, the fund has been more conservative with their dividends and appears to be slowing down the growth plans.

Private credit is feeling the pain. The immediate impact on publicly traded vehicles has been negative price movement ahead of negative impacts to book value. In the case of ARCC, this means the company’s premium price has vanished, and today, the fund trades in line with book value.

Enthusiastic investors who dove into the fund a year ago are now underwater as they wait for the valuation to expand or net asset value to catch up to their original target.

Looking more materially to investors, what would be the impact on ARCC’s dividends if the Fed actually decided to dramatically lower short term borrowing costs?

The ARCC dividend is exposed to falling SOFR due to its floating-rate loan exposure. After accounting for interest rate floors and hedges, roughly 80% of earnings remain sensitive to benchmark rates. With annualized net investment income (NII) of about $2.00 per share, small Fed cuts are unlikely to force a dividend change, assuming there are no dramatic credit events. The dividend risk becomes more tangible when we begin to look at deeper interest rate cuts. For example, a 100 basis point cut to the federal funds rate could cause net investment income to fall below $1.80 per share, meaning the dividend would no longer be covered.

The silver lining is that the company could shift around their capital structure to take advantage of the rapid change and thus alleviate the impact to earnings. Any way you slice it, it appears as though the tide is receding on BDCs.

Conclusion

From my perspective, this highlights the importance of choosing an operator that you believe will adeptly navigate credit challenges. Looking at ARCC, the fund’s manager, ARES, is one of the best in the business. Compared to newer operators, ARCC can leverage long-standing client relationships to do business extraordinarily well. Although somewhat unconventional, I believe relationships are important in real estate and lending. Having a long-term positive relationship can lead to better financial outcomes through trust and customary practices.

Pricing alongside book value, I rate ARCC a Buy below $20 per share. The company has fallen significantly and is prepared to face systemic credit challenges through their diversified portfolio and corporate toolkit. While macroeconomic challenges face these funds, their valuations have become fairer.

data.title: Madison Small Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The fourth quarter was another challenging quarter. We continued to underperform our benchmark, the Russell 2000, closing out an extremely disappointing 2025. The bull market in small caps continued, with the Russell 2000 closing up 2.2%, while the Madison Small Cap Fund (Class I) was down 0.4%. The Russell 2500 ended the quarter up 2.2%. Unlike previous quarters, sector allocation hurt performance much more than stock selection. From a sector perspective, Healthcare was the worst performing sector, driven by poor performance from our largest investment position, Encompass Health Care (EHC). Although the fundamentals for EHC are steady, EHC is both a defensive business and a defensive stock in a market that wants offense, a theme we'll discuss later in depth. Our best performing sector was Information Technology, driven by very strong stock selection, specifically stocks driven by the AI capital spending boom like Ciena (CIEN) and the takeover of Confluent (CFLT) by IBM (IBM) for a 35% premium.

It was another strong year for the Russell 2000, up 12.8%, although small caps continued their underperformance versus large caps, with the S&P 500 up nearly 18% for 2025. After a banner year in 2024, we underperformed our benchmark significantly in 2025. We are deeply disappointed by these results. Let's recap and analyze what happened.

The year began in turmoil, with threats to severely cut government spending via DOGE. Stocks exposed to government spending (ex defense) took a beating, particularly areas exposed to research in life sciences. The uncertainty around the impact of these cuts led to a broader selloff, with the Russell 2000 declining 9.8% and the Fund outperforming, but only slightly.

Things took a turn for the worse when the Trump administration began formulating plans for a new tariff regime, culminating in the draconian "Liberation Day" headline Tariff rates, which were more punitive than expected. The market plummeted and finally bottomed out early in Q2 as the administration backtracked on the most onerous Tariff rates. But the damage was done. Although the broader markets, along with the Treasury bond market, sold off sharply, companies with global supply chains took the brunt of the hit. Portfolio companies like VF Corporation (VFC) and Revolve Group (RVLV) which were two of our strongest stocks in 2024, erased all their gains.

Once the early April bottom was set, equities rallied sharply, buoyed by better-than-expected macro-economic data, particularly regarding inflation and employment. In the second quarter, the Russell 2000 regained nearly all its initial losses, up 8.5% for the quarter and down only 1.8% YTD. The Madison Small Cap Fund lagged the Russell 2000, returning 4.3% for the second quarter. The rally accelerated in the third quarter as did our underperformance, exposing the nature of the rally. The strength of the Russell 2000 was mostly due to an insatiable appetite for risk. The best performing stocks were the most speculative stocks: companies with no profits and often no revenue, but with thematic linkage to the capital spending boom in AI were in demand. This extended to the biotech sector, which had underperformed for several years. With no profits and often no revenue, biotechs generally outperform in more speculative markets when investors reach for risk. Even meme stocks were back, as was crypto and crypto-related companies. The IPO window reopened. Another curious aspect of the rally was the strength in mining stocks. Gold miners, silver miners, even crypto miners. Unfortunately for us, our philosophy and process keep us away from these speculative 'fishing ponds'.

We did have several poor performing stocks this year, which only exacerbate the underperformance. In our experience, markets like the one we had in 2025, even with flawless performance, might have delivered mediocre results. In the following paragraphs we'll autopsy what went wrong from a stock selection perspective and review the few things that went right to glean some insights into what 2026 might bring.

The epicenter of our underperformance was our Consumer Staples investments, where poor selection meaningfully hurts our performance. In our opinion the overarching reason for this weakness was a deeply out-of-favor sector. Investors simply do not have an appetite for defensive businesses and stocks in a pro-cyclical, speculative bull market. Historically, Consumer Staples companies, which range from food and beverage to personal care businesses, have carried a premium valuation to the market as investors were willing to pay a premium for stable and defensive nature of these companies. However, over the past several years that premium has contracted, and staples now trade at a historically steep discount. This has compressed valuations for the entire sector. Simply put, investors are now so used to consistent earnings and returns from cyclical stocks, such as the Magnificent 7, that the historically defensive names that are much slower growing, are no longer in vogue, but deeply out of favor. Some might call this exuberance or complacency, but that's a different discussion.

Furthermore, perceived challenges and headwinds are adding fuel to the fire in the staples sell off. These challenges include, but are not limited to: input cost inflation, the rise of GLP-1s, cuts to SNAP benefits, supply chain impacts from tariffs, and a stretched consumer. We say 'perceived' only because it's curious to us that none of these headwinds are cited when investors discuss Consumer Discretionary stocks, businesses that often face the exact same challenges. In an environment where investors generally want nothing to do with defensive businesses, any flaw in fundamentals, no matter how minor, leads to exaggerated downside volatility.

One of our bigger positions in Consumer Staples, Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG) delivered generally solid fundamentals all year, yet the stock was still down nearly 8% for the year. This was not a particularly expensive stock heading into 2025.

On the other hand, longtime portfolio holding Primo Brands (PRMB), saw its stock price cut in half. We acquired PRMB back in 2020. We like the water delivery business, we believe it exhibits all the traits we look for in a quality business. PRMB is one of the largest, if not the largest direct water delivery companies in North America, serving 5 gallon bottled water to consumers and small businesses via a modest monthly subscription or direct exchange. They also own some very attractive consumer retail brands like Mountain Valley and Saratoga Springs. In 2022 PRMB was acquired by Blue Triton Holdings which was the rebranded Nestle Water business and privately held. Blue Triton acquired Primo via a reverse merger, allowing it to go public. The combination created North America's largest water delivery and consumer retail franchise and potentially $200 million in cost savings, along with noncore asset sales that would clean up the balance sheet. The company delivered on expanding margins with steady topline growth and the market rewarded the company with a much higher valuation, making this one of our better performing stocks in 2023 and 2024. In 2025, the company decided to accelerate the integration and pull forward the cost savings. This proved to be a poor decision and during the summer of 2025 the company faced several logistical challenges with late or missed deliveries and subscription cancellations. This was exacerbated by a tornado that damaged one of their critical bottling plants in Texas. Although unfortunate, the mistakes were nowhere near fatal and new management has been quick to right the ship. However, the stock has been vaporized, erasing two years' worth of gains.

We believe the business is on track to deliver on all its opportunities from the combination and the current stock price represents an excellent investment opportunity.

Our second most disappointing sector was Information Technology, which on average, represents just over 25% of our investments. Simply put, we did not own enough stocks that were tied to the AI capital spending boom. Although most of these opportunities exist in the large cap space, the few that are in our small cap investment universe, we believe, do not fit the rigorous quality parameters our process demands. We also owned far too many software stocks. A significant portion (~10%) of our tech investments are application and infrastructure software companies. We generally like the software business model: high margins, high return on capital, recurring revenues, with significant defensive moats. However, with the rise of generative AI, many investors fear application software will be displaced as the data underpinning these applications will migrate to small or large foundational models, enabling rapid creation of custom solutions, reducing reliance on pre-built applications and lowering barriers to entry for competitors. It can commoditize core functionalities, making differentiation harder and accelerating disruption in established software markets. Similar to what's happened in Consumer Staples, investors have collapsed valuation multiples for most of the entire subsector. This placed pressure on all software valuations, save a few darlings like Palantir.

While this is an alluring thesis, our research leads to a vastly different conclusion. We do not believe that enterprises will take the data that underpins their mission critical applications—some of which are systems of record—and migrate them to language models that are currently generating “hallucinations”, a funny industry term for “errors”, at a rate as high as 60%, anytime soon. We've judiciously used this sell-off to our advantage. For example, CFLT, one of infrastructure software investments, reported a slightly disappointing quarter. The disappointment, in our opinion, was largely cosmetic. The market, however, severely punished the stock, treating the mild disappointment on one key performance indicator as if it was existential. We disagreed and used the opportunity to make CFLT one of our biggest positions, believing this to be a very strategic asset. Late in the fourth quarter we were validated when IBM announced a bid to acquire the company for a 35% premium.

In the fourth quarter, we took advantage of these depressed valuations to make two new investments in application software, Gitlab and Amplitude, names we'll discuss in detail later. There was a similar story in our semiconductor related investments. If the investment case had ties to Data Center capital buildout, like FormFactor (FORM), the stock did well. If the investment case rested largely on the health of the broader economy like Entegris (ENTG) and Power Integrations (POWI), then the stock languished.

Finally, we should discuss a standout performer in our Information Technology stocks, Ciena (CIEN). Ciena is a legacy telecommunication equipment company with leading technology in optical networking. Optical networking gear allows data to be transmitted through fiber optic cables. Historically, the company sold equipment to the likes of Verizon, ATT, Deutsche Telecom and other global service providers. Over the past several years, as companies like Meta, Microsoft, AWS (Amazon Web Services), and Netflix built massive data centers to deliver their own content, they began to build networks similar to the legacy service providers which required Ciena's equipment. This “cloud provider” business has grown now to over 50% of Ciena's revenue mix. Meta has been working closely with CIEN to develop technology for their massive data center buildout. They've agreed that CIEN can market this technology to the other data center players. This represents significant incremental opportunity for CIEN, and as the market recognized this, the stock has been rewarded with a significant premium multiple that's elevated the market cap to almost $35 billion, forcing us to sell this long-time investment holding.

Although Healthcare was technically a drag to our performance, this was mostly due to the strength in Biotech stocks. Biotech investing within our quality and valuation framework is a difficult, though not impossible universe to invest in. We recently added Vericel (VCEL) an investment that sits in the intersection of med tech and biotech. Although our Healthcare stocks delivered strong results in aggregate for the year, they couldn't keep pace with the strength of biotechs in our benchmark.

Portfolio Activity

In the fourth quarter we sold four stocks (WAL, CFLT, CCOI, CIEN) and added six new investments (MIR, VCEL, AMPL, VIAV, GTLB, AAON).

Sales:

Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL)

We sold our investment in Western Alliance upon finding the company was exposed to the First Brands bankruptcy via a note-on-note lending line to Jeffries/Point Bonita Capital. Our worst-case scenario analysis led us to conclude that there was a small but real probability of needing to raise capital. Furthermore, Utah-based Zions Bancorporation (ZION) disclosed a $50m charge-off on two defaulted commercial and industrial (C&I) loans. The loans totaled $60 million and were found to involve apparent misrepresentations, contractual defaults, and other irregularities. Zions initiated legal action against the borrowers and guarantors after internal reviews. Connected to the same borrower, WAL disclosed that it had a note finance revolving credit facility with Cantor Group V, LLC, which appears to be linked to the same borrower involved in Zions' troubled loans. WAL filed a lawsuit in August alleging borrower fraud, specifically for failing to provide collateral loans in the first position. Despite the lawsuit, WAL stated that it believes the existing collateral sufficiently covers the obligation. Credit fears could be an overhang on the shares for an extended period. Despite the recent price weakness, this has been a very successful investment for us. Recall, we bought WAL post the Silicon Valley Bank crisis.

Confluent, Inc. (CFLT)

We exited our investment in infrastructure software vendor CFLT following the announcement that IBM would acquire the company for $11 billion, or $31 per share, representing a ~35% premium to the previous Friday's closing price. We do not expect competing bids and decided to sell our position at prices over $30 per share to fund other new opportunities.

Cogent Communications Holdings (CCOI)

We decided to exit our position in CCOI. Our thesis hinged on the company cutting its dividend, a move widely anticipated by the market, and reestablishing growth in EBITDA. These combined moves would have reversed several quarters of increased leverage following the acquisition and integration of assets from T-Mobile. Instead, the company focused on EBITDA and was forced to suspend its dividend temporarily. Although we believe the dividend suspension is the right move, without that valuation ballast, the stock will trade on an uncertain EBITDA multiple. Without this ballast and combined with elevated leverage on the balance sheet, small variations in the multiple can lead to extreme swings in the equity value. We've decided to step back and wait until leverage improves.

Ciena Corporation (CIEN)

After its June quarter report and strong outlook, long-time portfolio holding Ciena was anointed as an AI capital spending beneficiary. With a market cap well over $30b and a very full valuation 44x 2026 price/earnings (P/E), we've decided to exit this position, as we believe better opportunities lie elsewhere. We've owned CIEN in various position sizes since 2010, but feel the market cap is now far too large for a small cap portfolio, even though fundamentals appear exceptional.

Buys:

Mirion Technologies, Inc. (MIR)

Mirion Technologies is a global leader in radiation detection, measurement, and monitoring solutions, serving both the nuclear and medical markets. The company is well-positioned to benefit from secular growth trends in nuclear power and cancer care. We like the recurring-revenue, consumption-based business model and healthy earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) margins (~25%), with room to expand to 30%. Mirion has all the hallmarks of a high-quality compounding with exposure to two secular growth markets. The company's expanding backlog and digital transformation initiatives position it for sustained, durable growth. There's been a recent renaissance in Nuclear energy with support across the political spectrum. We believe nuclear energy will play an important role in power generation globally at the intersection of green energy and the power-hungry AI revolution. The moats appear substantial, especially in nuclear technology. Mirion has a presence in 95% of nuclear power plants globally and a +60% market share in ionization radiation detection and monitoring in a tightly regulated market. Maintenance and upgrade cycles are the most important revenue driver, as parts and subsystems are almost always replaced like-for-like. We estimate the intrinsic value to be $30.

Vericel Corporation (VCEL)

Vericel is a medical device company specializing in cartilage repair and burn care. VCEL's autologous cartilage repair product is differentiated and value-added for younger patients with knee cartilage defects of 2-4 cm. It is a superior procedure to microfracture or other cartilage patch products, as it uses patients' own cartilage cells, which are cultured into a custom patch that differentiates into full-thickness cartilage. This is a niche application, but adoption is growing among sports medicine and orthopedic physicians. The company's burn care solution is also well-regarded in the market and is growing well. Sales at VCEL have grown at a mid-teens rate over the last five years. We think sales can accelerate slightly from this level with the next-generation product recently approved by the FDA. The company recently swung to profitability, and we see margin expansion as revenue ramps. Our intrinsic value estimate is $50 per share.

Amplitude Inc. (AMPL)

Amplitude has undergone a significant turnaround that has gone largely unnoticed since new CFO Andrew Casey joined the company to reboot the sales force and manage the company for profitable growth and free cash flow. Amplitude is a leader in digital analytics software that tracks, analyzes, and predicts user behavior across digital products (web and mobile). Its software helps product teams understand which features drive engagement, retention, and revenue. The economic quality is characterized by a highly recurring service revenue model, strong gross margins, and a recent inflection toward positive free cash flow. The company's primary protective moats are the extremely high switching costs for customers. Migrating a deeply embedded analytics system involves re-tagging all events, retraining teams, and potentially losing historical data, making it costly and disruptive. The balance sheet is pristine with no debt and almost $270m in cash. The company went public during the 2021 software Initial Public Offering (IPO) frenzy at an extremely high valuation and has been largely forgotten over the last few years as it has undergone a turnaround. Andrew Casey's resume is very impressive, with a background in tech giants Hewlett-Packard and ServiceNow. We believe the current valuation is extremely attractive and estimate the intrinsic value to be $21.

Viavi Solutions (VIAV)

Small cap initiated a new investment in the test and measurement company Viavi Solutions (VIAV). Viavi's test and measurement solutions provide network test, monitoring, and assurance solutions for telecommunications, cloud, enterprise, aerospace, defense, and industrial markets. The company operates through two primary segments: Network and Service Enablement (NSE): Offers test and measurement instruments, software, and services for wireless, wireline, cloud, data center, and military markets. Optical Security and Performance Products (OSP): Provides optical coatings and components used in anticounterfeiting, 3D sensing, automotive, and industrial applications. Test and measurement is a very sticky business with substantial moats, making strategic mergers and acquisitions an important driver of growth and moat expansion. In early 2024, Viavi Solutions made an initial $1.3 billion bid to acquire Spirent Communications, a UK-based provider of network testing and assurance solutions. Shortly after, Keysight Technologies countered with a higher bid of $1.46 billion, which Spirent accepted. The U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) raised antitrust concerns, noting that Keysight and Spirent together controlled 85% of the high-speed Ethernet testing market, 60% of the network security testing market, and 50% of the radio frequency channel emulation market. To resolve these concerns, the DOJ required Keysight to divest its overlapping business units to Viavi under a consent decree. This deal closed on October 16, and we believe the Spirent assets will strengthen Viavi's position in the data center ecosystem and accelerate growth and margin expansion. We believe the market does not yet fully appreciate Viavi's vast opportunity in the data center market. We estimate the stock's intrinsic value to be $30.

GitLab Inc. (GTLB)

We initiated a new investment position in GitLab (GTLB), a Dev/Sec/Ops (development/security/operations) software platform vendor that helps software developers plan, build, and monitor code for new and existing applications. GitLab's strategic software tools help developers manage the entire software development pipeline: From the planning stage to the building, deployment, and monitoring stage, while keeping proprietary software secure through the entire development lifecycle. The company has attractive mid-teens operating margins, a pristine balance sheet, and is growing at a double-digit rate in a very large and growing market. GitLab coexists in an attractive duopoly with Microsoft's GitHub platform. We have invested alongside the recently appointed CEO, Bill Staples, in the past and believe he's an excellent steward of capital. The stock has been under severe pressure for some time, largely due to the belief that AI-powered "Vibe Coding" software companies will displace GTLB solutions. We fundamentally reject this thesis and believe that as software development becomes easier and more productive, more code will be generated, increasing the need for planning, development, and monitoring tools like GitLab's, and will eventually make GitLab more, not less, valuable. We estimate GTLB's intrinsic value is $75 per share and that current prices reflect an attractive investment opportunity.

AAON, Inc. (AAON)

AAON is a provider of rooftop HVAC units for non-residential construction and climate control solutions for data centers. The company is known for its high-quality custom and semi-custom solutions and is strong in the hospital, education, and data center end markets. The company's legacy HVAC solutions are augmented by the 2021 acquisition of BASX, which is well-positioned in data center cooling. We like the timing of this investment, as the company's core business has been somewhat challenged in 2025 due to an EPA-required refrigerant changeover that disrupted sales and a necessary but painful enterprise resource planning (ERP) rollout that delayed lead times. Nonetheless, we believe AAON has a strong brand in rooftop HVAC and has gained market share in this space over the past 10 years. We think 2026 will be better for legacy AAON. Additionally, the BASX data center cooling business is accelerating rapidly, and we believe it will scale to >$1B in revenue, driving accelerated earnings growth. We estimate intrinsic value at $114, implying that the stock is currently trading at about a 23% discount.

Outlook

We are hopeful that the speculative market has run out of steam, although the old adage that “markets can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent”, keeps us up at night. One thing we can say for certain is that the market’s insistence on speculative growth at any price means high quality businesses with durable moats can be found at attractive prices again. Many of these are already core portfolio holdings that underperformed last year.

There are many things to keep an eye on 2026: A new Fed chair, fiscal stimulus, midterm elections, and continued geopolitical volatility. Other questions we are constantly asking: How long will the AI capital spending cycle last? Will investors begin asking for return on this investment? Has inflation finally been addressed? What about employment? No matter what the answers to these questions are, they are not in our hands. What we can control is fundamental research on a company-by-company level, focusing on quality, durable business models with formidable moats. 2025 was a terrible year performance wise. We take solace in the fact that our mistakes were not existential and that our underperformance was mostly due to the texture of the market. While we can’t predict the future, we have bounced back from difficult periods like this before and will work hard to rebuild performance.

Respectfully,

Faraz Farzam, Aaron Garcia
